personal.name: Amjad Rehawi
personal.tagline: Hardware engineer for things that move themselves.
personal.subTagline: Senior honors double major (EE + ME) at Santa Clara University, pivoting an EV power-electronics stack into autonomous drone and aerospace hardware.
personal.degree: B.S. Electrical Engineering & B.S. Mechanical Engineering (Honors)

brandThesis.headline: The same stack that runs an EV — battery, BMS, thermal, control, sensors — runs a drone.
brandThesis.body: I'm building the hardware spine for autonomous flight. Cells, busbars, BMS, motor drives, IMU and lidar fusion, vehicle control. The work I've done on FSAE traction packs and STMicro motor-drive validation maps directly onto eVTOL propulsion, swarm coordination, and aerospace-grade power electronics. Raven is the proof — an 8-stage roadmap from single-tilt-rotor airframe to a 4-drone autonomous swarm.

about.heading: About
paragraphs: Senior honors student at Santa Clara University, graduating June 2027 with a double major in Electrical and Mechanical Engineering. 3.8 GPA. I learn by building the full stack — schematic, PCB, firmware, mechanical, control — and shipping the result to a hard deadline.

Over the past two years I've designed a 600 V FSAE traction pack with a custom slave-board BMS, validated motor-drive ICs at STMicroelectronics in a precision lab environment, and won the SCU CrossFit Maker Challenge with an embedded heart-rate device that pushed accuracy from 76% to 93%. Each project sharpened a different layer of the same vertical: cells, power electronics, sensing, firmware, control.

Raven is where it all converges. An eVTOL roadmap I'm running solo through 2028 — Stage 1 builds the airframe and tilt-rotor mechanism, Stages 4-6 layer in IMU/lidar autonomy, Stages 7-8 prove out a 4-drone autonomous swarm. The thesis is simple: the hardware engineering needed for safe autonomous flight is the hardware engineering I've been doing on cars, and I'm here to do it on aircraft.

projects.name: Raven
projects.subtitle: An 8-stage solo roadmap from tilt-rotor eVTOL to a 4-drone autonomous swarm.
projects.summary: Raven is a self-directed eVTOL program. I'm building it the way a startup would build a flight vehicle: hardware first, vertically integrated, on a fixed timeline. Eight stages, each with a deliverable I can fly or measure. The end state is a coordinated 4-drone swarm running shared mission state on aerospace-grade autonomy hardware.

hero.pitch: EV-stack hardware engineer translating battery / BMS / drive / control experience into aerospace.
highlights: Custom 6S Li-ion pack + BMS with cell balancing and thermal cutoffs
Tilt-rotor mechanism with redundant servo control
STM32-based flight controller with sensor fusion (IMU + barometer + GPS)
Stage 4-6: lidar + visual-inertial odometry, full GNC stack
Stage 7-8: ROS2 mesh + shared mission state across 4 vehicles

sections.heading: Why I'm building this
sections.body: Recruiters at Skydio, Anduril, Shield AI, Zipline and AeroVironment all want hardware engineers who can own a propulsion / autonomy stack end-to-end. The fastest way to prove I can do that is to actually do it — solo, on a clock, in public. Raven is the proof artifact.

sections.heading: Hardware scope
items: Airframe: carbon-tube boom, glass-fiber wing skin, 3D-printed nacelle housings
Propulsion: 3× outrunner BLDC + KISS24A ESCs, two tilting wing-tip rotors + one fixed tail rotor
Power: 6S 22.2 V Li-ion pack, custom slave-board BMS, INA228 power telemetry
Compute: STM32H7 flight controller + Jetson Orin Nano companion for vision
Sensing: ICM-42688P IMU, MS5611 baro, u-blox F9P RTK, Livox MID-360 lidar (Stage 5+)

sections.heading: Stage status (May 2026)
sections.body: Stage 1 is in progress — airframe CAD complete, first carbon boom cut and bonded, tilt-rotor servo mechanism printed and bench-tested. Stage 2 power-system spec is drafted; long-lead cells are on order.

roadmap.title: Airframe + tilt-rotor mechanism
roadmap.desc: Carbon-tube boom, V-tail, two-axis tilt mechanism with redundant servos.

roadmap.title: Power system + BMS
roadmap.desc: 6S Li-ion pack, custom slave-board BMS, INA228 telemetry, thermal cutoffs.

roadmap.title: Flight controller bring-up
roadmap.desc: STM32H7 board, IMU + baro + GPS fusion, manual hover.

roadmap.title: Autonomous hover + waypoint
roadmap.desc: RTK GPS + barometer altitude hold, mission planner integration.

roadmap.title: Lidar + visual odometry
roadmap.desc: Livox MID-360 + Jetson Orin VIO, indoor flight without GPS.

roadmap.title: Full GNC stack
roadmap.desc: Closed-loop guidance, obstacle avoidance, automated transition to forward flight.

roadmap.title: 2-drone coordination
roadmap.desc: ROS2 mesh networking, shared world state, formation flight.

roadmap.title: 4-drone autonomous swarm
roadmap.desc: Coordinated mission execution, dynamic role assignment, fault-tolerant handoff.

projects.name: Bronco Racing FSAE
projects.subtitle: 600 V traction pack and slave-board BMS for SCU's first electric Formula SAE car.
projects.summary: I led the high-voltage subsystem on Bronco Racing's first electric FSAE car. Designed and built a 600 V traction pack with a custom slave-board BMS, 144 thermistors, cell-level balancing, and an isolation-monitoring loop that meets FSAE EV rules.

hero.pitch: 600 V traction pack + custom BMS, designed from cell-spec to assembly.
highlights: 144 NTC thermistors across 12 segments, isolated CAN telemetry to the VCU
Cell-level balancing with passive discharge and per-cell voltage sensing
Pack-level isolation monitor and pre-charge / discharge circuit
Bus-bar layout and pack mechanicals released through SOLIDWORKS PDM

sections.heading: What I owned
items: Slave-board BMS schematic and PCB layout (Altium)
Cell-selection trade study (Molicel P42A vs LG M50T)
Bus-bar CAD and welding fixture design
FSAE EV rules-compliance audit for the HV subsystem

sections.heading: Lessons that map to drones
sections.body: The cell-spec → BMS → telemetry → pack-mechanical chain is the same chain a drone power system runs, just at lower voltage. The discipline of treating the pack as a safety-critical subsystem — isolation monitoring, fault response, thermal envelope — is exactly what an eVTOL pack needs.

projects.name: STMicroelectronics
projects.subtitle: Motor-drive IC validation engineer (intern), Santa Clara CA.
projects.summary: Validation engineering intern in STMicro's motor-drive IC group. Built automated bench setups to characterize gate-driver behavior across temperature and supply variation, and authored test reports that fed directly into product-release sign-off.

hero.pitch: Bench-validation of motor-drive ICs across PVT corners with automated data capture.
highlights: Designed Python + VISA test rigs to sweep VCC, temperature, and gate-drive load
Characterized propagation delay, dead-time, and shoot-through margin on prototype silicon
Authored validation reports used in product-release reviews

sections.heading: What I learned
sections.body: Pre-silicon assumptions break in lab. The discipline of designing a test to falsify a datasheet number — not confirm it — is the discipline I bring to every hardware bring-up now.

projects.name: PalmSync
projects.subtitle: Embedded heart-rate device that won the CrossFit Maker Challenge.
projects.summary: A wrist-worn heart-rate monitor that uses pressure-compensated PPG sensing and motion-artifact rejection to hit clinical-grade accuracy during heavy lifting. Pushed accuracy from a 76% baseline to 93% in real-world CrossFit conditions.

hero.pitch: Wrist wearable with pressure-compensated PPG that survives heavy lifting.
highlights: Custom analog front-end with adjustable LED current and TIA gain
On-device DSP for motion-artifact rejection (Kalman + spectral tracking)
Accuracy: 76% → 93% on CrossFit workout dataset
Won the SCU CrossFit Maker Challenge

projects.name: Build-A-Bronco
projects.subtitle: Junior-design course capstone — embedded vehicle build and demo.
projects.summary: ENGR 172 capstone: a small autonomous ground vehicle that completes a timed obstacle course. Owned the powertrain and control stack — motor selection, H-bridge driver board, line-following and ultrasonic-avoidance firmware on an STM32 Nucleo.

hero.pitch: Autonomous obstacle-course vehicle, designed in 10 weeks.
highlights: Custom H-bridge driver board for two brushed DC motors
PID loop for line-following with HC-SR04 ultrasonic obstacle override
Mechanical chassis printed and bonded to the PCB stack

projects.name: CrossFit Maker Challenge
projects.subtitle: Patent-pending wearable that earned the team-of-three top prize.
projects.summary: PalmSync's underlying motion-artifact rejection technique was novel enough to file. Provisional patent submitted with two co-inventors. Won the SCU CrossFit Maker Challenge.

hero.pitch: Wearable IP filed off the back of a hackathon win.
highlights: Provisional patent on pressure-compensated PPG with adaptive motion rejection
Co-authored with two SCU engineering peers
Pitched to SCU's Ciocca Center for Innovation & Entrepreneurship

bullets: Built Python + VISA test rigs to sweep VCC, temperature and gate-drive load across PVT corners on prototype motor-drive silicon.
Characterized propagation delay, dead-time, and shoot-through margin; authored validation reports cited in product-release sign-off.
Designed and laid out a daughterboard for thermal stress testing that became the team's standard bench fixture.
Designed and built the team's first 600 V traction pack — cell trade study, bus-bar layout, mechanical pack assembly.
Owned the slave-board BMS schematic and PCB (Altium): 144 thermistors, per-cell voltage sensing, cell-level passive balancing.
Wrote the HV subsystem rules-compliance package and presented at SCU's Engineering Design Showcase.
Led a three-person team to first place with PalmSync, a pressure-compensated PPG wearable.
Designed the analog front-end and on-device DSP that lifted heart-rate accuracy from 76% to 93% in CrossFit conditions.
Filed a provisional patent on the motion-rejection technique with two co-inventors.
